text: The surgeon places a large synthetic mesh flat over the abdominal wall defect to reinforce the area, ensuring the mesh remains smooth and free from wrinkles or deformation.
The surgeon carefully dissects the space between the peritoneum and the posterior rectus sheath, taking care due to the thin and fragile nature of the peritoneum.
The surgeon performs dissection in the preperitoneal space, carefully reducing the hernia sac from the surrounding subcutaneous tissue while preserving the integrity of the skin.
The surgeon continues dissection along the midline, where adipose tissue is visible between the peritoneum and the linea alba, facilitating easier separation of these structures.
The surgeon clears the hernia orifice, visually inspecting the area to identify any additional defects in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon continues the dissection on the contralateral side, carefully navigating the anatomical layers to maintain separation between the mesh and intestines.
The surgeon encounters a thin and fragile peritoneum during dissection, which is prone to damage and the formation of small buttonholes.
The surgeon creates a large preperitoneal space, preparing the area for mesh placement to repair the hernia.
The surgeon closes the hernia orifice tightly using a permanent continuous self-locking suture, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
The surgeon applies multiple suture passes to evenly distribute tension across the tissue and thread, ensuring secure closure of the hernia repair site.
The surgeon measures the margins around the hernia orifice to confirm sufficient space for adequate mesh overlap, ensuring proper coverage during the repair.
The surgeon measures seven centimeters laterally from the midline on both sides in preparation for mesh placement in a three-centimeter hernia repair.
The surgeon inserts the mesh into the preperitoneal space, ensuring it covers the hernia orifice with adequate overlap.
The surgeon applies a self-fixating polyester mesh over the closed hernia orifice, eliminating the need for additional fixation methods.
The surgeon introduces a 15 by 15 cm mesh into the dissected space, ensuring it fits adequately within the prepared area for hernia repair.
